column1: Timeline of Brennus and the Celtic Invasions
Brennus (Leader of the Senones, Sack of Rome)

column3: Details
The Senones, led by Brennus, attack Clusium, an Etruscan city, after a dispute. The Romans intervene, angering the Gauls.
Brennus defeats the Roman army at the Allia River, marking one of Rome's greatest defeats.
Brennus and the Senones enter Rome and plunder the city. The Romans take refuge on the Capitoline Hill.
When the Romans offer gold to end the siege, Brennus famously throws his sword onto the scales, demanding more tribute.

column2: End of the Sack of Rome
column3: According to legend, Marcus Furius Camillus leads a Roman counterattack, forcing the Gauls to withdraw.
Accounts vary—some say he retreats to Gaul, others that he is killed in battle.

column1: Brennus (Leader of the Gauls, Invasion of Greece)

column3: Details
Celtic tribes move southward from Central Europe, threatening the Greek world.

column2: Brennus Leads Gallic Invasion of Greece
column3: Brennus becomes the leader of a large Gallic force and marches into Greece.
Brennus attacks the Greek coalition at Thermopylae, eventually bypassing their defenses.
Brennus leads his forces to plunder the Oracle of Delphi, seeking its legendary riches.
The Greeks, aided by a sudden snowstorm and rockslides, repel the Gauls. Some sources claim divine intervention.
Wounded in battle and unable to recover, Brennus reportedly takes his own life.
The remnants of Brennus' army later become the Galatians of Asia Minor.
Details
Roman historians depict Brennus as a ruthless barbarian, symbolizing Rome’s vulnerability.
Greek sources claim Apollo sent a storm to punish the Gauls for attacking the sacred site.
In medieval chronicles, Brennus is sometimes merged with later Celtic warrior figures.

column1: I'll expand the timeline with more details and events while maintaining the same table structure:
Timeline of Brennus and the Celtic Invasions
Brennus (Leader of the Senones, Sack of Rome)

column3: Details
Senones and other Gallic tribes cross the Alps and settle in northern Italy, establishing a presence in the Po Valley region
The Senones, led by Brennus, attack Clusium, an Etruscan city, after a dispute. The Romans intervene, angering the Gauls
Roman ambassadors, sent to mediate at Clusium, break their neutrality and join the battle against the Gauls, provoking Brennus to march on Rome
Brennus defeats the Roman army at the Allia River on July 18, marking one of Rome's greatest defeats
Most citizens flee Rome, with only a small garrison remaining on the Capitoline Hill
Brennus and the Senones enter Rome and plunder the city for several months. The Romans take refuge on the Capitoline Hill
Sacred geese in the Temple of Juno alert Romans to a Gallic night attack on the Capitoline Hill, saving the last Roman stronghold
When the Romans offer gold to end the siege, Brennus famously throws his sword onto the scales, demanding more tribute

column2: End of the Sack of Rome
column3: According to legend, Marcus Furius Camillus leads a Roman counterattack, forcing the Gauls to withdraw
Brennus and his forces withdraw from Roman territory, taking their plunder back north
Romans rebuild their city with hasty, unplanned construction, leading to Rome's infamous narrow streets
Accounts vary—some say he retreats to Gaul, others that he is killed in battle or dies of illness

column1: Brennus (Leader of the Gauls, Invasion of Greece)

column3: Details
Large Celtic tribes move southward from Central Europe into Thrace and Macedonia, threatening the Greek world
Celts defeat Macedonian forces under Ptolemy Keraunos, who is killed in battle
Brennus consolidates power among the Celtic tribes and proposes an invasion of rich Greek sanctuaries
Greek city-states form an alliance to defend against the Celtic invasion
Brennus gathers approximately 152,000 infantry and 20,400 cavalry (according to Pausanias, likely exaggerated)
Celtic forces enter Greece through Thessaly, pillaging as they advance
Brennus attacks the Greek coalition at Thermopylae. After initial failure, he finds a path around the Greek position, similar to the Persian maneuver in 480 BCE
Brennus divides his army, sending Acichorius with part of the force to raid Aetolia, drawing away Greek defenders
Aetolians successfully defend their territory, inflicting heavy casualties on Celtic raiders
Brennus leads approximately 40,000 warriors toward Delphi and its legendary treasures
Greeks harass the advancing Celtic forces with skirmish tactics in the mountains
Brennus leads his forces to plunder the Oracle of Delphi, seeking its legendary riches
The Greeks, aided by a sudden snowstorm and rockslides, repel the Gauls. Some sources claim divine intervention by Apollo
Wounded in battle and unable to recover, Brennus reportedly takes his own life by drinking undiluted wine or falling on his sword
Acichorius leads the surviving Celts northward, harassed by Greek forces

column2: Surviving Gauls Cross to Asia Minor
column3: Upon invitation from Nicomedes I of Bithynia, remaining Celtic forces cross the Hellespont
The remnants of Brennus' army later become the Galatians, settling in central Anatolia
Details
Roman historians depict Brennus as a ruthless barbarian, symbolizing Rome's vulnerability
The sack of Rome leads to significant military reforms and the construction of the Servian Walls
July 18, the date of the Battle of Allia, becomes marked as a day of ill omen in the Roman calendar
Greek sources claim Apollo sent a storm to punish the Gauls for attacking the sacred site, with appearances of divine warriors
Fear of Celtic warriors influences Greek military strategy and art, with the emergence of "Dying Gaul" sculptures
Galatians preserve Celtic language and customs in Anatolia for several centuries
St. Paul writes his Epistle to the Galatians, addressing descendants of these Celtic settlers
In medieval chronicles, particularly Geoffrey of Monmouth, Brennus is sometimes merged with later Celtic warrior figures
Renewed interest in Livy and Polybius brings the Brennus stories back to prominence
Both Brennus figures become symbols of Celtic resistance against Mediterranean powers in modern nationalist narratives

column1: Expanded Timeline of Brennus and Celtic Prehistory (2000 BCE - 279 BCE)
Proto-Celtic Origins and Bronze Age (2000-800 BCE)

column3: Details
Early Bronze Age culture in Central Europe develops sophisticated metallurgy, possibly proto-Indo-European speaking
Burial mound tradition spreads across Central Europe, showing early cultural unification
Cremation burial practices spread across Europe; considered a possible proto-Celtic cultural phase
Extensive trade routes link Central Europe with Mediterranean civilizations, introducing new technologies and cultural practices
Cooler, wetter conditions in Europe lead to population movements and cultural adaptations
Linguistic evidence suggests the differentiation of Proto-Celtic from other Indo-European languages during this period
First defensive settlements appear in Central Europe, indicating increasing social complexity and conflict

column1: Early Iron Age and Hallstatt Culture (800-450 BCE)

column3: Details
Iron technology becomes widespread in Central Europe, revolutionizing agriculture and warfare
Social stratification increases with emergence of warrior elites controlling trade and resources
Greek and Etruscan luxury goods begin appearing in elite Celtic burials, indicating trade connections
Major fortified settlement along the Danube becomes a center of Celtic culture and Mediterranean trade
Development of elaborate "princely" burials indicating concentration of wealth and power
Spectacular elite female burial in Burgundy region demonstrates extensive Mediterranean connections
Traditional Hallstatt centers decline, possibly due to shifting trade routes or internal social changes

column1: La Tène Culture and Celtic Expansion (450-300 BCE)

column3: Details
New artistic style and cultural practices develop in Celtic Europe, named after discoveries at La Tène, Switzerland
Increased population and social pressures lead to outward migration from Central Europe
Early Celtic groups cross the Alps and settle in the Po Valley, encountering Etruscan civilization
The Senones, a Celtic tribe from the area of modern eastern France, move into northeastern Italy

column1: Brennus I: Early Life and Rise (Fictional/Speculative Based on Celtic Customs)

column3: Details
Born into a noble Senones family in Transalpine Gaul (modern eastern France)
Raised in warrior traditions with emphasis on physical prowess and martial skills
Son of a respected warrior-chief named Brianos (speculative), who led his clan in early migrations
According to Celtic custom, sent to foster with allied family to learn leadership and warrior skills
Participates in first raids against neighboring tribes, displaying leadership abilities
Marries daughter of influential Senones chief, strengthening his position within the tribe
Participates in tribal council decision to migrate across the Alps seeking new lands
Leads a portion of the Senones tribe across the Alps into the Po Valley of northern Italy
Establishes Senones settlements in what becomes known as Cisalpine Gaul
Following his father's death or retirement, emerges as chief of the Senones in Italy

column1: Brennus I: The Roman Conflict (391-385 BCE)

column3: Details
Conflict begins with Etruscan city of Clusium over land and resources
Romans send Fabii brothers as ambassadors to mediate the dispute
Roman ambassadors violate neutrality by joining battle against Gauls
Sends messengers to Rome demanding the Fabii be handed over for their violation
Rome refuses to punish the Fabii, instead electing them as military tribunes
Brennus gathers war council and decides to punish Rome directly
Leads approximately 30,000 warriors southward toward Rome
Ambushes and crushes Roman army at the Allia River, opening path to Rome
Finds Rome largely abandoned except for the Capitoline Hill
Besieges last Roman defenders on the Capitoline Hill for several months
When weighing gold tribute, throws his sword on scales saying "Woe to the vanquished"
Leaves Rome with plunder, either due to disease, food shortages, or threats to their homeland
Distributes plunder, strengthening his position among Celtic tribes in northern Italy
Consolidates Senones territory in northeastern Italy
Disappears from historical record, possibly died of natural causes or in subsequent conflicts

column1: Celtic World Between the Two Brennus Leaders (385-280 BCE)

column3: Details
Romans rebuild their city and begin to develop stronger military organization
Celtic tribes in northern Italy form loose confederations for mutual defense
Roman military reforms partially in response to Gallic threat
Italian Gauls begin serving as mercenaries for various Mediterranean powers
Celtic art and craftsmanship reaches its peak in homeland territories
Alexander meets with Celtic ambassadors who claim to fear only that "the sky might fall"
Small Celtic groups begin probing the northern Balkans
Celtic groups expand into what is now Hungary, Romania, and Serbia
Romans engage in wars with Etruscans, sometimes employing Celtic mercenaries
Powerful Celtic kingdoms established along the Danube River
Rome secures dominance over central Italy, bringing them closer to Gallic territories
After death of Lysimachus, Macedonian kingdom experiences succession struggles
Large confederation of Celtic tribes gathers on the northern borders of Greece

column1: Brennus II: Early Life and Rise (Fictional/Speculative Based on Celtic Customs)

column3: Details
Born into a powerful clan of the Prausi or Tectosages tribe in Danubian region
Raised during period of tribal migration and warfare in Eastern Europe
Son of successful war leader who expanded tribal territory eastward
Undergoes traditional training in Celtic warrior arts
Participates in raids against Illyrian and Thracian peoples
Gains reputation through successful raids and personal combat
Commands personal war band (comitatus) attracting warriors with success and plunder
Becomes chief of his tribe through combination of military success and political alliances
Promotes idea of invasion of Macedonia and Greece, citing their wealth and internal divisions
Successfully unites various Celtic tribes under his leadership for southern campaign

column1: Brennus II: The Greek Invasion (280-279 BCE)

column3: Details
Leads invasion force of multiple Celtic tribes southward into Thrace
Celtic forces defeat Macedonian army, kill King Ptolemy Keraunos
Some Celtic groups under Cerethrius and Bolgius raid Thrace and Macedonia
Winters in captured territories, gathers intelligence on Greek defenses
Organizes largest Celtic army yet seen in the region (claimed 152,000 infantry by ancient sources)
Leads forces into Thessaly, defeating local resistance
Greek states form defensive alliance against Celtic invasion
First attack on Greek position at Thermopylae is repulsed
Discovers mountain path around Thermopylae (similar to Persian invasion route)
Sends Acichorius with part of army to raid Aetolia as diversion
Leads approximately 40,000 warriors toward Delphi Oracle and its treasures
Greek forces harass Celtic army with skirmish tactics in mountainous terrain
Attacks sanctuary of Delphi but faces fierce Greek resistance
Massive storm with lightning, rockslides, and earthquake disrupts Celtic attack
Severely wounded during battle at Delphi
Unable to recover from wounds, reportedly takes his own life
Surviving forces under Acichorius retreat northward, harassed by Greek forces

column1: Aftermath and Celtic Legacy (278 BCE - Later Influences)

column3: Details
Nicomedes I of Bithynia invites Celtic mercenaries to cross to Asia Minor
Approximately 20,000 Celtic survivors cross the Hellespont
Celts serve Nicomedes in his civil war, gaining reputation as fierce warriors
Seleucid king Antiochus I defeats Galatians using war elephants
Celtic tribes settle in central Anatolia, establishing the region later known as Galatia
Attalus I of Pergamon defeats Galatians, commissioning famous "Dying Gaul" sculptures
Romans defeat Cisalpine Gauls in Italy, beginning final conquest of Po Valley
Romans under Manlius Vulso invade Galatia, subjugating the region
Pompey reorganizes East, making Galatia a Roman client state
Julius Caesar publishes his "Gallic Wars," shaping Roman view of Celts
Emperor Augustus converts Galatia into a formal Roman province
St. Paul visits and writes Epistle to the Galatians
Roman historian Tacitus records information about Celtic and Germanic peoples
Medieval chronicler incorporates Brennus into legendary British history
Romantic nationalist movements revive interest in Celtic heritage
Modern archaeology reveals actual extent and sophistication of Celtic civilization

column1: Comprehensive Timeline of Ancient Migration and Celtic History

column3: Details
Migration from the Middle East to Europe begins after the last ice age (needs confirmation)
Ancient Celtic ancestors arrive in the British Isles and Western Europe from the Steppes; white skin genetics arrive in Europe with Middle Easterners and Central Asians (needs confirmation)

The Yamnaya (Pit Grave/Ochre Grave) culture develops between the Southern Bug, Dniester, and Ural rivers in the Pontic–Caspian steppe; characterized by distinctive burial traditions in tumuli (kurgans) containing simple pit chambers
Mikhaylovka, in lower Dnieper river, Ukraine, formed the Core Yamnaya culture
Details
Hurrians established in the Near East region; King Tupkish of Urkesh (with a Hurrian name) recorded on clay sealing
First recorded inscription of Hurrian language by Tish-atal, king of Urkesh
Early Bronze Age culture in Central Europe develops sophisticated metallurgy, possibly proto-Indo-European speaking
Details
In Biblical record, Jacob and his family migrate to Egypt due to famine in Canaan
Canaanites, including future Hyksos, migrate to Egypt due to famine
Gradual influx of Semitic peoples into Egypt's eastern Delta region
Burial mound tradition spreads across Central Europe, showing early cultural unification

Emergence of Mitanni state (Hanigalbat/Hani-Rabbat in Assyrian records, Naharin in Egyptian texts) in northern Syria and southeast Anatolia with Hurrian-speaking population and Indo-Aryan linguistic influences
At its maximum extent, Mitanni ranged from Kizzuwatna by the Taurus Mountains in the west to Tunip in the south, Arraphe in the east, and north to Lake Van
Details
The Hyksos control northern Egypt before being expelled and migrating northward; theorized to have carried Mesopotamian religious symbols into Central Asia
Mitanni becomes a strong regional power, limited by the Hittites to the north, Egyptians to the west, Kassites to the south, and later by the Assyrians to the east
Prince Moses leads the Exodus of Hebrews who recognize Abraham
Expulsion of remaining Hyksos from Egypt to Canaan
Period of enslavement for Israelites remaining in Egypt, according to some interpretations
